notes: “NA” denotes that an estimate is not reported.
1. Total fertility rate is expressed per woman aged 15-49 years.
2. Rates are for the period 1-36 months preceding the interview.

sections.heading: Fertility moved lower overall
sections.body: The Philippines recorded a total fertility rate of 1.7 children per woman in 2025, down from 1.9 in 2022. The decline also appears in the general fertility rate, which fell from 63 to 57, and in the crude birth rate, which fell from 13.3 to 11.6.

sections.heading: Regional differences remain large
sections.body: The 2025 regional range runs from 1.3 children per woman in CALABARZON to 2.4 in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao. Zamboanga Peninsula follows at 2.3, with its 2025 figure including Sulu, while Caraga and MIMAROPA are also above 2.0.

sections.heading: The highest-fertility regions declined most
sections.body: Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao fell from 3.1 in 2022 to 2.4 in 2025, and SOCCSKSARGEN fell from 2.5 to 1.9. Among regions with comparable values, the gap between the highest and lowest regional fertility rates narrowed from 1.9 children per woman in 2022 to 1.1 in 2025.

sections.heading: Fertility is concentrated in the late twenties
sections.body: Age-specific fertility in 2025 peaks at ages 25 to 29, with 94 births per 1,000 women, followed by ages 30 to 34 at 84 and ages 20 to 24 at 67. The largest decline from 2022 was at ages 20 to 24, where the rate dropped by 17 points.

sections.heading: Rural fertility remains higher at every main age
sections.body: Rural women had a 2025 total fertility rate of 2.0, compared with 1.5 among urban women. The rural advantage is especially visible at ages 20 to 24, where the rate is 84 versus 56, and at ages 25 to 29, where it is 108 versus 85.

sections.heading: Education divides are steep
sections.body: Women with Grades 1 to 6 had the highest 2025 fertility rate at 3.1 children per woman, while women with college education had the lowest at 1.3. The sharpest change since 2022 was among women with Grades 11 to 12, whose rate fell from 2.7 to 1.8.

sections.heading: Wealth shows the clearest social gradient
sections.body: Fertility rises steadily as wealth falls: 1.1 children per woman in the highest wealth quintile, 1.3 in the fourth, 1.7 in the middle, 2.1 in the second, and 2.8 in the lowest. This pattern suggests, as an inference, that the national average masks very different family-size realities across economic groups.

takeaways: National fertility fell to 1.7 children per woman in 2025 from 1.9 in 2022.
Regional fertility is highest in BARMM at 2.4 and lowest in CALABARZON at 1.3.
Fertility is most concentrated at ages 25 to 29, followed by ages 30 to 34.
Rural, lower-education, and lower-wealth groups continue to show higher fertility.
